title: Arithmetic on Markoff Surfaces
speaker: Daniel Martin
abstract: The generalized Markoff equation gives rise to a dynamical system via the Markoff group acting on the solution set. This talk considers the resulting dynamics over finite fields, which has strong connections with combinatorial group theory and number theory. Specifically, we focus on the orbit structure of the Markoff group action, which McCullough and Wanderley conjecture to be trivial outside of a small number of exceptional orbits. Cases of this conjecture have been verified in recent work of Chen and of Bourgain–Gamburd–Sarnak. We will present new techniques that establish the McCullough–Wanderley conjecture in additional settings.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Classical Markoff equation and Vieta tree
proof_stages.summary: Markoff's study of the spectrum of minima of indefinite binary quadratic forms leads to integer triples satisfying the Markoff equation, generated from an initial solution by Vieta involutions and forming a connected tree over the integers.

proof_stages.label: Strong approximation as finite-field graph connectivity
proof_stages.summary: Sieve questions about divisibility, squarefreeness, and almost-primality of Markoff numbers require finite-field densities to reflect integer triples, which is reformulated as connectedness of the Markoff graph over F_p and, for stronger sieve results, as expansion.

proof_stages.label: Fricke-Klein trace coordinates and Nielsen graphs
proof_stages.summary: Representations of the once-punctured torus into PSL_2 have trace coordinates satisfying the Markoff equation, and elementary Nielsen moves on generating pairs induce the same coordinate permutations and Vieta involutions as the Markoff graph action.

proof_stages.label: Classification and q-classification equivalence
proof_stages.summary: The Nielsen classification conjecture asks matrix-pair graphs at fixed commutator trace to be as connected as possible, the q-classification conjecture translates this to connectedness of essential trace triples, and McBeath's analysis of the two SL_2-conjugacy classes over each trace triple supplies the crossing needed to prove the conjectures equivalent, though not their expansion analogues.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain-Gamburd-Sarnak and Chen orbit-hopping strategy
proof_stages.summary: Coordinate-stabilizer orbits are organized by an associated eigenvalue order; maximal orbits form a large cage, Corvaja-Zannier bounds allow smaller sufficiently large orbits to hop toward larger ones, and Chen's theorem that all orbit sizes are 0 mod p eliminates remaining components for all sufficiently large primes.

proof_stages.label: Linear-algebra dual method for q-classification
proof_stages.summary: Each orbit is encoded by a vector counting first-coordinate frequencies, q-classification is reduced to computing the dimension of the span of these vectors, and orthogonal vectors are generated from orbit-sum-zero polynomials via reductions to univariate polynomials, giving finite-computation criteria such as the verified divisors up to 19.

examples.name: The kappa = 3 failure of naive lifting
examples.note: The fact that many finite-field kappa=3 triples exist but no integer triples lift is used as a counterexample that motivates replacing naive strong approximation by the essential-triples formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: kappa=3 is cited as a counterexample where nothing lifts to Z despite many finite-field triples, without deriving the failure.

passes.reason: The kappa=3 surface is not structurally exceptional here, but it refutes naive lifting because its finite-field triples have no integral lifts.
For kappa=3 the integral lifting set is empty, so it directly defeats the naive lifting version of strong approximation.
For κ=3 the integral lift set is empty despite many finite-field triples, refuting the naive lifting generalization.
